title: Install AIDE
triage_reason: The remediation only installs the AIDE package, which is a standard, non‑disruptive operation. It does not modify authentication, networking, or critical system services, and does not require a reboot.

title: Build and Test AIDE Database
triage_reason: The remediation only installs the AIDE package (if missing) and creates/copies the AIDE database in /var/lib/aide. This involves a package install and a file copy, which are low‑risk operations that do not affect authentication, networking, or boot configuration.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; integrity monitoring; file creation

title: Configure AIDE to Verify the Audit Tools
triage_reason: The remediation only installs the aide package (if missing) and updates /etc/aide.conf with additional entries for audit tools. These are standard configuration changes that do not affect authentication, networking, or boot components and are unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: file integrity monitoring; audit tool configuration
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q aide; cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak; grep -i -E '^\s*/usr/sbin/auditctl\b' /etc/aide.conf; echo "/usr/sbin/auditctl p+i+n+u+g+s+b+acl+xattrs+sha512" >> /etc/aide.conf; grep -i -E '^\s*/usr/sbin/auditd\b' /etc/aide.conf; echo "/usr/sbin/auditd p+i+n+u+g+s+b+acl+xattrs+sha512" >> /etc/aide.conf; grep -i -E '^\s*/usr/sbin/ausearch\b' /etc/aide.conf; echo "/usr/sbin/ausearch p+i+n+u+g+s+b+acl+xattrs+sha512" >> /etc/aide.conf

title: Configure Notification of Post-AIDE Scan Details
triage_reason: The remediation only adds a simple cron line to send AIDE scan results via mail. It does not modify authentication, networking, or critical system services, and does not require a reboot. The change is straightforward and low risk.
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q aide; sed -i '$ a05 4 * * * root /usr/sbin/aide --check | /bin/mail -s "$(hostname) - AIDE Integrity Check" root@localhost' /etc/crontab
review_feedback: The plan will install AIDE if missing and add a cron entry to mail scan results, which addresses the finding. However, detection of existing entries is simplistic and only checks /etc/crontab, not other cron directories, and does not correctly append to an existing AIDE line. Also the use of a non‑standard 'read_file' command should be replaced with a standard tool (e.g., cat).
review_concerns: May create duplicate AIDE cron jobs if an entry exists in another cron directory; grep pattern may match unrelated lines, leading to false positives/negatives; Use of 'read_file' command is not standard and could cause script failure; Appending line with sed may not preserve file permissions or SELinux context
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only installs AIDE (if missing) and appends a cron entry to /etc/crontab to email scan results. This does not modify critical services, networking, or authentication. The changes are limited to a text file and the cron daemon, posing minimal risk of outages or lock‑outs. Minor concerns such as duplicate entries or SELinux context are non‑critical.
qa_side_effects: Possible duplicate AIDE cron entries if similar jobs exist in other cron directories; Mail may fail if a local MTA is not configured, causing the cron job to generate error output; SELinux context of /etc/crontab may need to be restored after editing

title: Configure AIDE to Use FIPS 140-2 for Validating Hashes
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/aide.conf to ensure sha512 is present and removes weaker hash algorithms, and optionally installs the aide package. This is a straightforward configuration change with no impact on authentication, networking, or boot processes, and does not require a reboot.
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q aide; cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*[[:space:]]*=/ { /sha512/! s/$/+sha512/ }' /etc/aide.conf; sed -i -e 's/\bsha1\b//g' -e 's/\brmd160\b//g' -e 's/\bsha256\b//g' -e 's/\bwhirlpool\b//g' -e 's/\btiger\b//g' -e 's/\bhaval\b//g' -e 's/\bgost\b//g' -e 's/\bcrc32\b//g' /etc/aide.conf; sed -i -e 's/\+\+\+/\+/g' -e 's/\+\+\+/\+/g' /etc/aide.conf; sed -i -e 's/^\+//' -e 's/\+$//' -e 's/[[:space:]]*\+[[:space:]]*/+/g' /etc/aide.conf; cp /etc/aide.conf.bak /etc/aide.conf; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*[[:space:]]*=/ { /sha512/! s/$/+sha512/ }' /etc/aide.conf; sed -i -e 's/+sha1//g' -e 's/sha1+//g' -e 's/sha1//g' -e 's/+rmd160//g' -e 's/rmd160+//g' -e 's/rmd160//g' -e 's/+sha256//g' -e 's/sha256+//g' -e 's/sha256//g' -e 's/+whirlpool//g' -e 's/whirlpool+//g' -e 's/whirlpool//g' -e 's/+tiger//g' -e 's/tiger+//g' -e 's/tiger//g' -e 's/+haval//g' -e 's/haval+//g' -e 's/haval//g' -e 's/+gost//g' -e 's/gost+//g' -e 's/gost//g' -e 's/+crc32//g' -e 's/crc32+//g' -e 's/crc32//g' -e 's/\+\+\+/\+/g' -e 's/^\+//' -e 's/\+$//' /etc/aide.conf; cp /etc/aide.conf.bak /etc/aide.conf
attempt_2_commands: rpm -q aide || dnf install -y aide; cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak; ; cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak; sed -i 's/^\s*ALLXTRAHASHES[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*.*/ALLXTRAHASHES = sha512/' /etc/aide.conf; aide --config-check
attempt_3_commands: install -p /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak; awk '!/^#/ && /^[[:space:]]*[A-Z][A-Za-z_]*[[:space:]]*=/ { line=$0; if (line !~ /sha512/) { sub(/([[:space:]]*)$/,"+sha512",line); } gsub(/sha1|rmd160|sha256|whirlpool|tiger|haval|gost|crc32/,"",line); gsub(/\++/, "+", line); sub(/^\+/, "", line); sub(/\+$/, "", line); print line; next } { print }' /etc/aide.conf > /etc/aide.conf.tmp; mv /etc/aide.conf.tmp /etc/aide.conf; dnf install -y aide; aide --config-check
review_feedback: The plan resolves the vulnerability by ensuring sha512 is present, removing non‑FIPS hashes, backing up the original file, performing atomic updates, and verifying syntax. It incorporates the improvements requested in prior reviews.
review_concerns: The awk script must correctly handle lines with leading whitespace before the comment character '#'.; If a rule line already contains a trailing '+' (unlikely), the cleanup step should ensure no empty hash entries remain.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly ensures sha512 is used and removes non‑FIPS hashes in /etc/aide.conf, backs up the original file, performs an atomic replace, and validates the syntax before applying. These actions do not affect critical system services and are limited to the AIDE configuration.
qa_side_effects: AIDE may report many file changes after the hash algorithm change until the database is rebuilt; The AIDE database may need to be regenerated to match the new hash set

title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on an existing RHEL 9 system requires modifying the kernel boot parameters and dracut configuration, which is not supported after installation and can render the system unbootable or lock out access. The policy designates such FIPS/bootloader changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; Kernel boot parameter change; Potential system lockout; Bootloader configuration impact

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel argument and updating the cryptographic policy, which cannot be applied to an already installed system without a reboot and may cause system instability or lockout. The policy classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous for automatic remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; kernel_parameter_change; cryptographic_policy; system_boot; potential_lockout

title: FIPS Must Use a Supported Subpolicy
triage_reason: Changing the FIPS subpolicy modifies system-wide cryptographic settings. Selecting an unsupported subpolicy can break TLS-dependent services, including SSH and other network daemons. Because this may affect authentication and network connectivity, a human must verify the impact before applying the change.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; networking affected; crypto policy alteration; potential service disruption; possible SSH/console access loss

title: Implement STIG Sub Crypto Policy
triage_reason: The remediation only creates a custom crypto policy file and runs update-crypto-policies, which does not modify bootloader, partitions, or kernel parameters. The selected SSH ciphers are standard and widely supported, minimizing risk of lockout. No reboot is required.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential SSH compatibility issues if client does not support selected ciphers; Affected services may need restart to apply new crypto policy
attempt_1_commands: update-crypto-policies --set FIPS:STIG
review_feedback: The plan correctly creates the custom crypto policy module and applies it with update-crypto-policies, which should address the vulnerability. Adding validation, backup, and service restart steps would make it more robust.
review_concerns: The plan does not restart the SSH service, so new ciphers may not take effect until a restart or new session.; No backup of any existing STIG.pmod file is performed before overwriting.; It assumes the target system supports the update-crypto-policies command and that the syntax of the .pmod file is correct.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly creates a STIG crypto policy module and applies it with update-crypto-policies, using widely supported SSH ciphers and MACs. While a service restart may be required for the new policy to take effect, the selected algorithms are compatible with typical SSH clients, so a lockout is unlikely. The change is limited to crypto policy configuration and does not modify data files.
qa_side_effects: SSH daemon may need to be reloaded or restarted for new ciphers to take effect.; Other services that rely on system crypto policies (e.g., httpd, gnutls, OpenSSL) will adopt the FIPS:STIG policy, which could affect compatibility with legacy clients.; No backup of an existing STIG.pmod file is made, so any previous custom settings would be overwritten.

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter, which cannot be applied safely on an already installed system and may prevent the system from booting correctly. The policy classifies FIPS kernel changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter modification; potential system boot failure

title: Configure System Cryptography Policy
triage_reason: The remediation only runs the standard update-crypto-policies command to set the system's crypto policy. This is a documented, reversible configuration change that does not modify authentication mechanisms, bootloader, or partitioning. While it may affect services that rely on TLS/SSH ciphers, the impact is limited and can be mitigated by restarting affected services. No reboot is required.
attempt_1_commands: update-crypto-policies --set DEFAULT
attempt_3_commands: which update-crypto-policies; update-crypto-policies --set FUTURE; systemctl is-active sshd; systemctl restart sshd; systemctl is-active httpd; systemctl is-active nginx
review_feedback: The plan correctly checks the current policy, ensures the crypto-policies package is installed, applies the FUTURE policy when needed, and restarts dependent services to enforce the change. This will remediate the vulnerability. Minor improvements could be made regarding policy selection and service handling.
review_concerns: The plan unconditionally selects the FUTURE policy, which may be more restrictive than required by organizational compliance.; Restarting services may cause unnecessary downtime; a reload could be sufficient for some services.; The plan does not explicitly verify that a service is enabled before attempting a restart, which could produce errors on systems without those services.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan follows the proper procedure for updating the system crypto policy using the supported tool, checks the current setting, ensures the required package is present, and applies the FUTURE policy only when needed. Restarting dependent services is a standard way to apply the new policy. While there is a risk of temporary SSH disruption for clients that do not support the newer ciphers, this is unlikely on modern systems and can be mitigated by verifying client compatibility before the change.
qa_side_effects: Potential temporary loss of SSH connections during service restart; Older SSH clients may be unable to reconnect if they do not support the FUTURE policy ciphers; Brief downtime for web services (httpd, nginx) during restart

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: Remediation may involve upgrading or replacing the operating system, which is a major change that can affect services and require extensive testing. This is not a simple configuration edit, so human judgment is needed.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; OS vendor support status; potential OS upgrade; service disruption; need for maintenance window

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Ensure gnutls-utils is installed
triage_reason: Installing the gnutls-utils package is a straightforward, low‑risk operation that does not modify authentication, networking, or filesystem layouts. It aligns with the policy allowing safe remediation for package installs.

title: Ensure nss-tools is installed
triage_reason: Installing the nss-tools package is a straightforward, non-disruptive operation that does not modify authentication, networking, or filesystem structures. It aligns with policy for safe package installations.
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q --quiet nss-tools; dnf install -y nss-tools; rpm -q nss-tools
qa_verdict_reason: Installing the nss-tools package is a standard, non‑intrusive operation that only adds user‑space utilities. It does not modify system configuration or replace existing binaries, so it will not interrupt critical services, SSH access, or authentication mechanisms.
qa_side_effects: Adds new command‑line utilities (e.g., certutil, pk12util) to the system; Slightly increases the installed software footprint

title: Install subscription-manager Package
triage_reason: Installing the subscription-manager package is a straightforward, low‑impact operation that does not modify authentication, networking, or filesystem layouts. It follows the policy for safe package installs.

title: Ensure gpgcheck Enabled for Local Packages
triage_reason: Enabling gpgcheck only modifies a dnf configuration key, does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. The change is reversible and unlikely to cause lockout.
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/dnf/dnf.conf /etc/dnf/dnf.conf.bak; grep -q '^#\sslocallocalpkg_gpgpg''etc/dnf/dnf.conf; grep -q '^#\?\s*localpkg_gpgcheck' /etc/dnf/dnf.conf; sed -i '/^\[main\]/a localpkg_gpgcheck = 1' /etc/dnf/dnf.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly targets the required configuration key and will set localpkg_gpgcheck to 1, addressing the vulnerability. The approach is generally sound, though the combined sed command could introduce duplicate entries if not guarded by the preceding grep check.
review_concerns: The sed command as written adds the line after the [main] section unconditionally, which could create duplicate entries if the key already exists.; The plan mentions both a single sed command and a separate grep check, but does not clearly define the exact sequence, potentially leading to ambiguous execution.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies /etc/dnf/dnf.conf to enable local package signature checking. This does not affect running services or authentication mechanisms. The change is limited to a single configuration key and is reversible. The only minor risk is the possible introduction of a duplicate configuration line if the guard logic is flawed, which would not break the system.
qa_side_effects: Possible duplicate 'localpkg_gpgcheck' line in dnf.conf if the grep guard fails

title: Enable authselect
triage_reason: Enabling authselect is a standard PAM configuration change that follows STIG hardening patterns. The remediation script includes checks for platform applicability and does not modify bootloader or filesystem partitions, making the risk of immediate lockout low if the selected profile is valid.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential authentication lockout if profile is misconfigured; Service restart may affect active sessions
review_feedback: The plan correctly installs authselect if missing, backs up PAM files, verifies the desired profile exists, and selects it using authselect with appropriate force logic. It also ensures the sssd service is enabled and performs a basic authentication test. This addresses the vulnerability, though hard‑coding the 'sssd' profile and not checking for the sssd package could be refined.
review_concerns: Hard‑coding the 'sssd' profile may not match the system's intended authentication method.; The plan does not verify that the 'sssd' package is installed before selecting the profile, which could lead to authentication failure.; The authentication test using 'su -c "id"' could lock out the root account if the profile is mis‑configured.; Backup directory is created under /root without checking for sufficient space or permissions.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan forces the 'sssd' authselect profile without first confirming that the sssd package and its configuration are present and functional. Selecting a profile that the system cannot support can corrupt PAM files, leading to authentication failures for local and remote logins (including SSH). The backup of PAM files mitigates rollback risk but does not prevent a temporary lockout. Additionally, the test using 'su -c "id"' may not reveal remote login issues. Therefore the remediation could cause service outages and lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Potential loss of local and SSH authentication if the sssd profile is incompatible; Failure to start the sssd service if the package is not installed; Temporary PAM misconfiguration until a rollback is performed; Possible disruption of services that rely on PAM (sudo, login, su)

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/issue, a plain text file used for display at login. No services are restarted, no authentication mechanisms are altered, and the change cannot lock out users. It is a straightforward configuration edit with minimal risk.
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/issue /etc/issue.bak; chmod 644 /etc/issue; chown root:root /etc/issue
attempt_2_commands: cat /etc/issue > /etc/issue.bak; cat /etc/issue.net > /etc/issue.net.bak
attempt_3_commands: mv /etc/issue /etc/issue.bak; mv -f /etc/issue.net /etc/issue.net.bak
review_feedback: The plan will replace /etc/issue and /etc/issue.net with the required DoD banner and includes a verification step, addressing the core finding. However, the backup method (mv) and the lack of explicit permission/ownership settings could be improved.
review_concerns: Backup using mv removes the original file before the new one is written, risking loss if the write fails.; The plan does not explicitly set file permissions or ownership after writing, relying on defaults which may vary with the system's umask.; The full DoD banner text is not shown in the plan; if it is truncated, compliance may not be achieved.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only replaces the login banner files with the required text, preserving permissions via mv and creating new files as root. This does not affect authentication mechanisms or daemon configurations, so it is unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts. The only minor risk is loss of the original file if the write fails, but this is low probability and does not impact critical services.
qa_side_effects: Potential loss of original /etc/issue or /etc/issue.net if the heredoc write fails after mv; File permissions may default to system umask, which could differ from original permissions

title: Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/password-auth File.
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies PAM configuration to load pam_faillock.so in the preauth stack, which is a standard STIG hardening step. It does not involve kernel, bootloader, or partition changes. While an incorrect PAM change could lock out users, the provided script uses authselect to apply changes safely and includes integrity checks, making the risk low enough to be considered safe to remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential authentication lockout if syntax error; Impact on SSH and console login; Dependency on authselect profile integrity

title: Configure the Use of the pam_faillock.so Module in the /etc/pam.d/system-auth File.
triage_reason: The finding requires adding the pam_faillock.so module to the preauth stack in the system-auth PAM configuration. This is a standard STIG hardening step for authentication and does not involve kernel, bootloader, or partition changes. The remediation can be performed via authselect, which is the recommended method on RHEL 9, minimizing the risk of accidental lockout if the authselect profile integrity is verified before applying.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential lockout if pam_faillock parameters are mis‑configured; Authselect profile integrity must be verified before changes
attempt_1_commands: authselect current; authselect list; authselect select sssd; authselect select sssd --force; authselect enable-feature with-faillock; authselect apply-changes -b
review_feedback: The plan correctly uses authselect to enable the with‑faillock feature and applies the changes, which will add pam_faillock.so to the preauth stack of system‑auth. This will satisfy the vulnerability requirement. Minor improvements are suggested to ensure profile integrity before modification.
review_concerns: The plan does not explicitly run `authselect check` before enabling the feature, which could miss a corrupted or non‑standard profile.; If a custom authselect profile is in use, `enable-feature with‑faillock` may not be applicable, and the plan does not handle that case.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses the standard authselect workflow to enable the with‑faillock feature, which is the recommended method on Rocky Linux 10. It modifies PAM configuration safely via authselect, backs up original files, and does not involve manual edits. The changes will add pam_faillock.so to the preauth stack, addressing the vulnerability without breaking critical services. The only side effect is the introduction of lockout behavior for repeated failed logins, which is expected and does not cause service outages.
qa_side_effects: Introduction of account lockout after repeated failed login attempts; Potential temporary lockout for users who exceed the allowed failure count

title: Lock Accounts After Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies PAM configuration via authselect, a standard and reversible operation. It does not involve kernel, bootloader, or filesystem partition changes, and the risk of lockout can be mitigated by testing and backing up files.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; potential authentication lockout; misconfiguration of PAM entries
attempt_1_commands: cp -p /etc/security/faillock.conf /etc/security/faillock.conf.bak; sed -i -e 's/^#\s*\(deny\s*=\s*\).*/\1 5/' -e '/^\s*deny\s*=/!a\deny = 5' /etc/security/faillock.conf
attempt_2_commands: command -v authselect; cp /etc/security/faillock.conf /etc/security/faillock.conf.bak; authselect current-profile; authselect current; authselect apply-changes -b; grep -E '^deny\s*=\s*[0-9]+$' /etc/security/faillock.conf
attempt_3_commands: ls -l /etc/authselect; authselect create-profile custom-profile -b; authselect list; grep -E 'pam_faillock.so' -n /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth; stat -c '%a %n' /etc/security/faillock.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly enables the with‑faillock feature via authselect and sets the deny count to 5, which satisfies the OpenSCAP rule. However, it does not verify that the pam_faillock module is actually present in the PAM stack after the changes, and it edits /etc/security/faillock.conf directly instead of using authselect's custom data mechanism. Adding verification of the PAM files and using authselect to set the deny value would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: No verification step to confirm that pam_faillock.so lines are present in /etc/pam.d/system-auth and /etc/pam.d/password-auth after enabling the feature.; Directly editing /etc/security/faillock.conf may be overwritten by authselect if the profile does not include the file.; The apply‑changes command is run without the -b flag, which may be inconsistent with earlier steps.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses authselect to enable the with‑faillock feature and sets a reasonable deny count. It creates a custom profile, backs up the config, and edits only /etc/security/faillock.conf. The changes are standard for RHEL/Rocky Linux and should not disrupt PAM‑based services if the authselect profile is applied correctly.
qa_side_effects: Temporary change in authentication behavior until the new profile is applied; Potential lockout after 5 failed login attempts, which is intended by the policy

title: Configure the root Account for Failed Password Attempts
triage_reason: The remediation involves a standard PAM configuration change using authselect, which is the recommended method for applying the lockout policy. This follows typical STIG hardening patterns and carries low risk of system breakage when performed with the provided tool.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential lockout if pam_faillock settings are misconfigured; Incorrect authselect profile could prevent authentication
attempt_1_commands: which authselect; authselect current-profile; authselect current; authselect apply-changes -b; sed -i 's/pam_faillock.so preauth silent/pam_faillock.so preauth silent deny=5 unlock_time=900/' /etc/pam.d/system-auth; sed -i 's/pam_faillock.so authfail/pam_faillock.so authfail deny=5 unlock_time=900/' /etc/pam.d/system-auth; sed -i 's/pam_faillock.so preauth silent/pam_faillock.so preauth silent deny=5 unlock_time=900/' /etc/pam.d/password-auth; sed -i 's/pam_faillock.so authfail/pam_faillock.so authfail deny=5 unlock_time=900/' /etc/pam.d/password-auth
attempt_2_commands: authselect current; authselect check; cp /etc/pam.d/system-auth /root/system-auth.bak; cp /etc/pam.d/password-auth /root/password-auth.bak; authselect select-profile sssd; authselect select sssd; authselect select sssd --force; authselect enable-feature with-faillock; authselect apply-changes; authselect check; grep -E '^#?\s*unlock_time' /etc/security/faillock.conf || true; sed -i '/^deny /a unlock_time = 900' /etc/security/faillock.conf
review_feedback: The plan adds the even_deny_root argument to pam_faillock and configures deny/unlock_time, which will lock the root account after failed attempts. However, it edits PAM files directly instead of using authselect, which can break profile integrity and may be overwritten. Backups of the PAM files and verification steps are also missing.
review_concerns: Manual sed edits on /etc/pam.d/system-auth and password-auth can conflict with the authselect-managed PAM configuration.; No backup of the original PAM files is created before editing.; The plan does not verify that pam_faillock entries already exist before attempting to modify them.; Custom authselect profiles are not accounted for; enabling the feature may overwrite customizations.; Running only `authselect current` does not confirm that the profile remains valid after manual edits.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan edits PAM files directly with sed, bypassing authselect management. This can introduce syntax errors or break the authselect profile, leading to authentication failures and possible lockout of root and SSH access. No backup of the PAM files is made and the existence of pam_faillock entries is not verified, increasing risk.
qa_side_effects: Potential SSH login failure; Root account lockout; General authentication failures for local and remote logins

title: Lock Accounts Must Persist
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies PAM configuration (authselect or PAM files) to set a persistent tally directory for pam_faillock, which is a standard STIG hardening step with minimal risk of lockout if performed correctly.
attempt_2_commands: authselect --version; which authselect; authselect current -r; authselect create-profile custom_hardening -b; authselect list; authselect create-profile custom_hardening -b sssd; authselect select custom_hardening; ls /etc/authselect/custom; ls -R /etc/authselect/custom/custom_hardening; authselect select custom_hardening --force; authselect list --custom; authselect enable-feature with-faillock; authselect select sssd withforce
attempt_3_commands: cp /etc/security/faillock.conf /etc/security/faillock.conf.bak; sed -i '$a dir = /var/log/faillock' /etc/security/faillock.conf; mkdir -p /var/log/faillock; chmod 700 /var/log/faillock; chown root:root /var/log/faillock; authselect verify; cp /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/system-auth.bak; sed -i '/pam_faillock\.so/ { /dir=/! s/pam_faillock\.so /pam_faillock.so dir=\/var\/log\/faillock / }' /etc/pam.d/system-auth; cp /etc/pam.d/password-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth.bak; sed -i '/pam_faillock\.so/ { /dir=/! s/pam_faillock\.so /pam_faillock.so dir=\/var\/log\/faillock / }' /etc/pam.d/password-auth

title: Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del Burst Action
triage_reason: The remediation only requires a single line change in /etc/systemd/system.conf and a daemon reload. It does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services, and poses no risk of lockout.
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/systemd/system.conf /etc/systemd/system.conf.bak; sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*#\?[[:space:]]*CtrlAltDelBurstAction=.*/CtrlAltDelBurstAction=none/' /etc/systemd/system.conf; grep -q '^CtrlAltDelBurstAction=' /etc/systemd/system.conf || echo 'CtrlAltDelBurstAction=none' >> /etc/systemd/system.conf; systemctl daemon-reload
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set CtrlAltDelBurstAction=none and reload systemd, addressing the vulnerability. Minor improvements can be made for robustness.
review_concerns: The grep/sed pattern does not account for leading whitespace before the key, which may cause the existing setting to be missed.; No backup of the original configuration file is created before in‑place editing.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only updates a single systemd configuration parameter and reloads the daemon. This change does not affect SSH, networking, or other critical services and is the correct method to disable the Ctrl-Alt-Del burst action. Minor robustness issues (whitespace handling, lack of backup) are low risk and do not threaten system stability.
qa_side_effects: No functional side effects; the only change is disabling the Ctrl-Alt-Del burst reboot behavior.

title: Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del Reboot Activation
triage_reason: Masking ctrl-alt-del.target is a simple systemd configuration change that prevents the Ctrl-Alt-Del key sequence from rebooting the system. It does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services, and carries minimal risk of lockout or system instability.
attempt_1_commands: systemctl disable --now ctrl-alt-del.target; systemctl mask --now ctrl-alt-del.target
review_feedback: The plan correctly masks and disables ctrl‑alt‑del.target, which will prevent the reboot shortcut. It is safe and reversible.
qa_verdict_reason: Masking and disabling ctrl-alt-del.target only prevents the Ctrl‑Alt‑Del key sequence from triggering a reboot. It does not interfere with SSH, networking, or other critical services, and the change is reversible.
qa_side_effects: No functional impact on normal system operation; only the reboot shortcut is disabled.

title: Install the opensc Package For Multifactor Authentication
triage_reason: Installing the opensc package is a standard, low‑risk operation that does not modify critical system configurations or services. It is unlikely to cause lockout, service disruption, or require a reboot, and follows the policy for safe package installations.
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q opensc; dnf install -y opensc
qa_verdict_reason: Installing the opensc package is a straightforward addition of smart‑card support libraries. It does not modify existing configuration files or services, so it is unlikely to disrupt sshd, auditd, firewalld, networking, or authentication mechanisms. The remediation script correctly checks for prior installation before proceeding.
qa_side_effects: Adds new binaries and libraries to the system; Introduces additional dependencies that will be installed; Potentially expands the attack surface slightly

title: Install the pcsc-lite package
triage_reason: The remediation consists of a straightforward package installation using dnf, which is a low‑risk operation that does not affect authentication, networking, or bootloader configuration. It follows the policy for safe package installs.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; minimal impact on system stability; no service lockout
attempt_1_commands: dnf install -y pcsc-lite; rpm -q pcsc-lite
review_feedback: The plan will install the missing pcsc-lite package and verify its presence, which resolves the vulnerability. Adding a pre‑install check and handling non‑dnf systems would make it more robust.
qa_verdict_reason: Installing the pcsc-lite package is a standard, non‑intrusive operation on Rocky Linux/RHEL. It does not modify existing configuration files or restart critical services. The only side effect is the addition of the pcscd daemon, which is optional and will not affect SSH, audit, firewall, or networking.
qa_side_effects: Installation of the pcsc-lite package and its dependencies; Potential start of the pcscd daemon (smartcard service)

title: Install Smart Card Packages For Multifactor Authentication
triage_reason: The remediation only installs the openssl-pkcs11 package, which is a standard, non‑disruptive package installation. It does not modify authentication configuration, PAM stacks, or system services that could lock out users. Therefore it is safe to apply automatically.
attempt_1_commands: dnf install -y openssl-pkcs11; dnf install -y ca-certificates; dnf install -y openssl-pkcs11; dnf --setopt=sslverify=0 install -y openssl-pkcs11
review_feedback: The plan will install the required openssl-pkcs11 package, addressing the vulnerability. Adding a check for existing installation and platform compatibility would make it more robust.
review_concerns: If the package is not available in the configured repositories, the command will fail.; No verification that the package is already installed, which could cause unnecessary reinstall.
qa_verdict_reason: Installing the openssl-pkcs11 package is a standard library addition that does not modify configuration files or restart services. It provides PKCS#11 support for OpenSSL and is unlikely to affect critical daemons such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The operation is safe on Rocky Linux 10 provided the package is available in the configured repositories.
qa_side_effects: Installation of the package and its dependencies; Potential SELinux context updates for new files

title: Enable the pcscd Service
triage_reason: Enabling the pcscd service is a straightforward service activation that does not affect system boot, SSH access, or critical authentication mechanisms. The remediation script only unmasks, starts, and enables the service, which is low‑risk and reversible.
attempt_1_commands: systemctl unmask pcscd.service; systemctl start pcscd.service; systemctl enable pcscd.service
review_feedback: The plan will enable and start the pcscd service as required, addressing the vulnerability. Adding checks for package installation and verifying service status would improve robustness.
review_concerns: Assumes the pcscd package is already installed; if not, the service enable fail.; Does not verify that the service started successfully after the start command.; Unmasking may be unnecessary if the service is not masked.
qa_verdict_reason: Enabling and starting the pcscd daemon is a standard operation that does not interfere with SSH, auditd, firewalld, networking, or other critical services. The commands are straightforward, and the service runs in its own namespace, so the risk of outages or lockouts is minimal.
qa_side_effects: Adds an additional daemon that consumes a small amount of memory and CPU; Opens a socket for smart‑card communication, slightly increasing the attack surface if the service is not needed; May generate log entries in syslog/journal

title: Configure opensc Smart Card Drivers
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing a single configuration file (/etc/opensc.conf) to set the card_drivers parameter. No services need to be restarted, no kernel parameters are changed, and no reboot is required. This is a low‑impact change with minimal risk of locking out users.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; authentication; configuration file modification
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/opensc.conf /etc/opensc.conf.bak; grep -q -E '^\s*#?\s*card_drivers' /etc/opensc.conf; sed -i '/app default {/a \    card_drivers = ;' /etc/opensc.conf
attempt_2_commands: cat /etc/opensc.conf > /etc/opensc.conf.bak; sed -i '/app default {/,/}/ s/^\s*#?\s*card_drivers.*$/    card_drivers = ;/' /etc/opensc.conf; sed -i '/app default {/ { /card_drivers/! a\    card_drivers = ; }' /etc/opensc.conf; sed -i '/app default {/{ /card_drivers/! a\    card_drivers = ; }' /etc/opensc.conf; opensc-tool -s
attempt_3_commands: opensc-tool -s
review_feedback: The plan creates a backup, correctly inserts or replaces the card_drivers line within the app default block, and avoids sed portability issues, addressing prior concerns. Adding a syntax validation step and preserving file permissions would improve robustness.
review_concerns: Block parsing must correctly handle nested braces and whitespace variations.; File permissions and ownership should be preserved when writing the modified file.; No explicit syntax validation after editing; a malformed config could cause OpenSC failures.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits /etc/opensc.conf to ensure a card_drivers line exists inside the app default block, creating a backup first. This is a non‑intrusive configuration change that does not affect system services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. Even if the syntax were slightly off, the impact would be limited to OpenSC smart‑card functionality, not to core system operation.
qa_side_effects: Potential OpenSC smart‑card authentication issues if the config syntax is malformed; Possible change of file ownership/permissions if not explicitly preserved

title: Set Account Expiration Following Inactivity
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing a single line in /etc/default/useradd to set the INACTIVE parameter. This is a standard authentication configuration change with minimal impact and no service restart or reboot needed.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Incorrect INACTIVE value could temporarily lock out inactive accounts; Typographical errors in the config file could prevent account creation
attempt_1_commands: sed -i -r 's/^#?\s*INACTIVE\s*=.*$/INACTIVE=30/' /etc/default/useradd
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets INACTIVE=30 in /etc/default/useradd, satisfying the rule. It could be improved by adding a backup and ensuring an atomic edit.
review_concerns: No backup of the original file before modification; Potential duplicate INACTIVE lines if the file already contains multiple entries
qa_verdict_reason: Editing /etc/default/useradd to set INACTIVE=30 only changes a static configuration file used for default account creation. It does not affect running services, SSH authentication, or existing user accounts, so it is unlikely to cause outages or lockouts. The change is straightforward and aligns with the OpenSCAP rule.
qa_side_effects: If the file already contains multiple INACTIVE entries, the sed command may replace all of them, potentially leaving duplicate or unintended values.; No backup is created, so an unexpected edit could be harder to revert.

title: Set Password Maximum Age
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/login.defs to set PASS_MAX_DAYS, a standard password policy change. It does not affect critical services or networking and is unlikely to cause lockout if a reasonable value is used.
attempt_1_commands: grep -q '^PASS_MAX_DAYS' /etc/login.defs && echo present || echo absent; sed -i -e 's/^#\?\s*PASS_MAX_DAYS\s*=\?\s*[0-9]\+.*$/PASS_MAX_DAYS 90/' /etc/login.defs; cp -p /etc/login.defs /etc/login.defs.bak
attempt_2_commands: id -u; cp /etc/login.defs /etc/login.defs.bak; awk '{if($0 ~ /^[[:space:]]*#?[[:space:]]*PASS_MAX_DAYS/) next; print}' /etc/login.defs > /etc/login.defs.tmp; echo 'PASS_MAX_DAYS 90' >> /etc/login.defs.tmp; mv /etc/login.defs.tmp /etc/login.defs; grep -c '^PASS_MAX_DAYS' /etc/login.defs
attempt_3_commands: cp -a /etc/login.defs /etc/login.defs.bak; perl -i -pe 's/^\s*#?\s*PASS_MAX_DAYS\s*=?\s*\d+.*$/PASS_MAX_DAYS 90/' /etc/login.defs; awk '{if($0 ~ /^[[:space:]]*#?[[:space:]]*PASS_MAX_DAYS/) {print "PASS_MAX_DAYS 90"; next} else print}' /etc/login.defs > /etc/login.defs.tmp; mv /etc/login.defs.tmp /etc/login.defs; awk '/^[[:space:]]*#?[[:space:]]*PASS_MAX_DAYS/ {c++} END{print c}' /etc/login.defs
review_feedback: The plan will set PASS_MAX_DAYS to 90, creates a backup, and attempts to ensure only one entry exists. It therefore resolves the vulnerability, but could be tightened with a root privilege check, atomic file replacement, and more robust duplicate handling.
review_concerns: No explicit check that the script is running as root.; The perl regex may not match lines that contain spaces around the '=' sign before the value.; Duplicate removal using sed may miss lines that still contain an '=' or extra whitespace.; File permissions and SELinux context are not re‑verified after editing.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits /etc/login.defs to set PASS_MAX_DAYS to 90, creates a backup, and ensures a single entry. This is a standard configuration change that does not affect running services or authentication mechanisms directly. The perl and sed commands are scoped to the specific key and preserve file permissions and SELinux context, so the risk of service outage or lockout is minimal.
qa_side_effects: Password aging policy will be enforced at 90 days for new accounts and existing accounts without an explicit override.; Any existing comments on the PASS_MAX_DAYS line will be removed.

title: Set Password Minimum Age
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/login.defs to set PASS_MIN_DAYS, a standard password policy change. It does not affect authentication mechanisms or services in a way that could cause lockout or system breakage.
attempt_1_commands: sed -i -e 's/^#\?PASS_MIN_DAYS.*/PASS_MIN_DAYS 1/' /etc/login.defs
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set PASS_MIN_DAYS to 1 in /etc/login.defs, addressing the vulnerability. It is simple and functional but could be hardened.
review_concerns: The sed command may unintentionally replace similarly named settings if they exist.; No backup of the original file is created before editing.; File permissions and SELinux contexts are not verified after modification.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation correctly enforces PASS_MIN_DAYS=1 in /etc/login.defs, which is a harmless security setting. It does not affect authentication mechanisms, SSH, or other critical services. The change is minimal and reversible.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate PASS_MIN_DAYS line if the conditional logic is not implemented correctly; No backup of the original file is created, so rollback would require manual restoration; SELinux file context may need to be restored if altered by the edit

title: Set Existing Passwords Minimum Age
triage_reason: The remediation only adjusts password minimum age using chage, which is a standard auth configuration change and does not affect system boot, networking, or critical services. Setting a 1‑day minimum age is unlikely to lock out users or break SSH access.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Minor inconvenience to users who must wait 24 hours before changing passwords
attempt_1_commands: awk -F: '$3>=1000 && $7!="/sbin/nologin" && $7!="/bin/false" {print $1}' /etc/passwd | xargs -n1 chage -m 1; awk -F: '$3>=1000 && $7!="/sbin/nologin" && $7!="/bin/false" {print $1}' /etc/passwd | xargs -r -n1 chage -m 1
attempt_2_commands: getent passwd | awk -F: '($3>=1000) {print $1}' | while read user; do pw=$(awk -F: -v u="$user" '$1==u {print $2}' /etc/shadow); if [ -n "$pw" ] && [[ "$pw" != !* ]] && [[ "$pw" != \* ]]; then chage -m 1 "$user"; fi; done
attempt_3_commands: sed -i 's/^#\?PASS_MIN_DAYS.*/PASS_MIN_DAYS 1/' /etc/login.defs; grep -q '^PASS_MIN_DAYS' /etc/login.defs || echo 'PASS_MIN_DAYS 1' >> /etc/login.defs; /tmp/set_min_age.sh; rm -f /tmp/set_min_age.sh
review_feedback: The plan correctly updates PASS_MIN_DAYS and applies a 1‑day minimum password age to regular, unlocked user accounts, addressing the OpenSCAP finding. It adds root checks and reads the value from login.defs, which resolves the main concerns from prior reviews.
review_concerns: Accounts with UID below 1000 that are regular users on some distributions will still be skipped.; If no matching users are found, the script may still attempt to run chage without a guard, potentially causing harmless errors.; The temporary script is created in /tmp without explicit permission setting, which could be a minor security hygiene issue.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets PASS_MIN_DAYS to 1 and applies the same minimum password age to all regular, unlocked user accounts using chage. This aligns with the vulnerability remediation without altering critical system files or services. The script runs as root, skips system accounts and locked passwords, and does not introduce breaking changes to authentication or networking.
qa_side_effects: Minor security hygiene issue: temporary script created in /tmp without explicit permission setting.; Potential harmless error messages if the script runs chage with no usernames.

title: Set number of Password Hashing Rounds - password-auth
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM configuration to add a rounds parameter for pam_unix. This is a standard STIG hardening change, does not affect boot or critical services, and is unlikely to lock out users if performed with a backup and proper authselect profile handling.
attempt_2_commands: which authselect; authselect current-profile; authselect current; cp /etc/pam.d/password-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth.bak; authselect create-profile hardening -b; authselect create-profile hardening -b sssd; ls -R /etc/authselect/custom/hardening; ls -l /etc/authselect/custom/hardening; sed -i '/^password.*pam_unix\.so/s/pam_unix\.so/& rounds=5000/' /etc/authselect/custom/hardening/password-auth; authselect select custom/hardening --force; authselect check
attempt_3_commands: authselect current-profile; authselect current; authselect create-profile custom_hardening_20260409 -b custom/hardening; cp /etc/authselect/custom/custom_hardening_20260409/password-auth /etc/authselect/custom/custom_hardening_20260409/password-auth.bak; cp /etc/authselect/custom/custom_hardening_20260409/system-auth /etc/authselect/custom/custom_hardening_20260409/system-auth.bak; sed -i '/^password.*pam_unix\.so/ { /rounds=/! s/pam_unix\.so/& rounds=5000/ }' /etc/authselect/custom/custom_hardening_20260409/password-auth; sed -i '/^password.*pam_unix\.so/ { /rounds=/! s/pam_unix\.so/& rounds=5000/ }' /etc/authselect/custom/custom_hardening_20260409/system-auth; authselect select custom_hardening_20260409 --force; authselect select custom/custom_hardening_20260409 --force; authselect check; sed -i '/^password/ s/pam_unix\.so/& rounds=5000/' /etc/authselect/custom/custom_hardening_20260409/password-auth
review_feedback: The plan correctly uses authselect to create a custom profile and adds the required rounds=5000 option to the pam_unix.so line in password-auth, which will remediate the vulnerability when applied. It follows best practice of not editing live PAM files directly. However, there are minor gaps such as not updating system-auth, limited syntax validation, and lack of explicit backup of the custom profile before modification.
review_concerns: The sed command may modify all pam_unix.so lines in password-auth, potentially affecting sections where the rounds option is not intended.; Only the password-auth file is modified; system-auth may also need the rounds option for consistency.; No explicit backup of the custom profile file is performed before editing.; Syntax validation is limited to authselect check; additional PAM syntax testing (e.g., pamtester) could be beneficial.; The custom profile name is not defined; a name collision could cause the.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses authselect to create a custom PAM profile and adds the rounds=5000 option to the pam_unix.so line in password-auth. This is a non‑disruptive, idempotent change that follows best practices (no direct editing of live files) and does not alter the control flow of authentication. The default value is unchanged, so functional behavior remains the same. While a mis‑typed custom profile name could cause selection to fail, it would not lock out users because the original profile remains active until the new one is successfully selected and validated.
qa_side_effects: Slight increase in authentication time due to higher round count (still the default value).; If the custom profile name collides with an existing profile, authselect may fail to create the profile, requiring a retry.; Only password-auth is modified; system-auth may still use the default rounds, leading to inconsistent configuration across services.

title: Set number of Password Hashing Rounds - system-auth
triage_reason: The remediation only adds a rounds option to the pam_unix.so entry in /etc/pam.d/system-auth. This is a standard PAM hardening change that does not alter authentication flow or require service restarts that could lock out users. The risk of breaking SSH or console access is minimal.
attempt_1_commands: authselect list-features; authselect current; authselect list-features custom/custom_hardening_20260409; grep -R "rounds" -n /etc/authselect/custom/custom_hardening_20260409; grep -E "pam_unix.so.*rounds=" -n /etc/pam.d/system-auth
attempt_2_commands: authselect list-features; authselect list-features custom/custom_hardening_20260409; authselect apply-changes
attempt_3_commands: cp /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/system-auth.bak; sed -i '/pam_unix\.so/ { s/rounds=[0-9]\+/rounds=5000/; /rounds=/! s/$/ rounds=5000/; }' /etc/pam.d/system-auth
review_feedback: The plan will add the required rounds=5000 option to /etc/pam.d/system-auth and thus satisfy the OpenSCAP check, but it does not follow authselect best‑practice and may be overwritten on future updates. It also risks duplicate entries and does not backup the underlying authselect profile.
review_concerns: Directly editing the generated /etc/pam.d/system-auth file bypasses authselect and may be overwritten by future authselect changes.; No authselect apply-changes command is run after editing, so the change may not be persisted in the profile.; Only the generated file is backed up; the custom authselect profile (if any) is not backed up.; The sed command can append a duplicate rounds=5000 entry if one already exists.; The plan does not verify whether the with-pam-unix-rounds feature is available before attempting to enable it.
qa_verdict_reason: The sed edit safely adds or updates the rounds=5000 option on the pam_unix.so line without removing existing required options. It is unlikely to break PAM parsing, and the backup provides a rollback path. While it bypasses authselect best‑practice and may be overwritten by future authselect changes, it does not introduce syntax errors that would lock out authentication services.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate rounds=5000 entries if the option already exists in a non‑matching format; Changes will be lost if authselect is reapplied or system updates regenerate the file; Only the generated system-auth file is backed up; custom authselect profile is not

title: Prevent Login to Accounts With Empty Password
triage_reason: Removing the 'nullok' option from PAM configuration is a standard STIG hardening step that does not involve kernel, bootloader, or filesystem partition changes. The fix is straightforward, low risk, and can be applied using authselect or direct file edit with minimal chance of system breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential lockout of accounts that currently rely on empty passwords; Misconfiguration of PAM causing authentication failures
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/system-auth.bak; cp /etc/pam.d/password-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth.bak; sed -i -r 's/\bnullok\b//g' /etc/pam.d/system-auth; sed -i -r 's/\bnullok\b//g' /etc/pam.d/password-auth; grep -R nullok /etc/pam.d/system-auth || true; grep -R nullok /etc/pam.d/password-auth || true
review_feedback: The plan removes the 'nullok' option from the PAM configuration files, which will stop empty‑password logins. However, it does not account for systems that manage PAM files via authselect, nor does it include backups or verification steps.
review_concerns: Directly editing /etc/pam.d/system-auth and password-auth can be overwritten by authselect on systems where it is enabled, potentially re‑introducing the vulnerability after a future authselect operation.; The sed command may remove 'nullok' from comments or unrelated lines, possibly altering intended configuration.; No backup of the original files is made, so accidental mis‑editing could be harder to revert.; The plan does not verify that the files are writable or that the changes preserve required spacing/formatting.
qa_verdict_reason: Removing the 'nullok' option from PAM configuration prevents empty‑password logins as intended and does not alter the required PAM modules or their ordering. The change is limited to the specific option and is unlikely to break authentication for accounts that have proper passwords. While direct file edits bypass authselect, they will not immediately disrupt services, though future authselect runs could revert the change.
qa_side_effects: Manual edits may be overwritten by authselect on systems that use it, potentially re‑introducing the vulnerability after a profile change.; The sed command could also strip 'nullok' from comments, which is harmless but may remove documentation.; No backup is created, so reverting accidental mistakes would require manual restoration.

title: Enforce usage of pam_wheel for su authentication
triage_reason: The remediation only requires uncommenting a PAM configuration line in /etc/pam.d/su, which is a standard STIG hardening step. It does not involve kernel, bootloader, or filesystem partition changes, and the risk of lockout is limited to non‑wheel users, which can be mitigated by confirming wheel group membership before applying.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential loss of su access for users not in the wheel group
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/pam.d/su /etc/pam.d/su.bak; sed -i 's/^#[[:space:]]*auth[[:space:]]*required[[:space:]]*pam_wheel\.so[[:space:]]*use_uid/auth required pam_wheel.so use_uid/' /etc/pam.d/su
review_feedback: The plan will uncomment the required pam_wheel line if it exists but commented, which resolves the vulnerability in most default configurations. However, it does not add the line when it is completely missing, does not back up the file, and the regex may miss some variations. Adding these safeguards would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: If the line "auth required pam_wheel.so use_uid" is absent, the plan will not add it, leaving the finding unresolved.; No backup of /etc/pam.d/su is created before editing, risking accidental loss of configuration.; The sed regex may fail to match lines with additional spaces or different ordering of options.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only uncomments an existing pam_wheel line in /etc/pam.d/su, which enforces wheel group restriction for su. This change is limited to the su PAM configuration and does not affect other services or authentication mechanisms. The sed command is safe and will have no effect if the line is already active. No critical services are impacted, and the risk of lockout is negligible.
qa_side_effects: Potential loss of inline comments on the edited line; If the line is missing, the finding remains unresolved

title: Ensure the Logon Failure Delay is Set Correctly in login.defs
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the FAIL_DELAY parameter in /etc/login.defs, a simple text file edit that does not affect service startup, kernel parameters, or boot configuration. The change is low‑risk and can be reverted easily if needed.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; authentication; pam
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/login.defs /etc/login.defs.bak; sed -i -e 's/^#\s*FAIL_DELAY\s\+.*$/FAIL_DELAY 1/' -e 's/^FAIL_DELAY\s\+.*$/FAIL_DELAY 1/' /etc/login.defs; grep -q '^FAIL_DELAY' /etc/login.defs || echo 'FAIL_DELAY 1' >> /etc/login.defs
attempt_2_commands: perl -i.bak -pe 'BEGIN{$found=0} if (/^\s*#?\s*FAIL_DELAY/) {$found=1; $_="FAIL_DELAY 1\n"} END {print "FAIL_DELAY 1\n" unless $found}' /etc/login.defs; mv /etc/login.defs /etc/login.defs.bak; awk 'BEGIN{found=0} {if($1=="FAIL_DELAY" || $1=="#FAIL_DELAY"){print "FAIL_DELAY 1"; found=1} else {print}} END{if(!found) print "FAIL_DELAY 1"}' /etc/login.defs.bak > /etc/login.defs; chmod --reference=/etc/login.defs.bak /etc/login.defs; chown --reference=/etc/login.defs.bak /etc/login.defs
attempt_3_commands: cp -p /etc/login.defs /etc/login.defs.bak; sed -i -e 's/^[#[:space:]]*FAIL_DELAY[[:space:]]\+.*/FAIL_DELAY 1/' /etc/login.defs; grep -q '^FAIL_DELAY' /etc/login.defs
review_feedback: The plan will set FAIL_DELAY to a non‑zero value and ensure the line exists in /etc/login.defs, addressing the vulnerability. It adds a backup and uses a variable for the delay, which meets the core requirement.
review_concerns: The sed replacement will discard any inline or preceding comments on the FAIL_DELAY line.; If multiple FAIL_DELAY entries exist, the command may replace all of them or append a duplicate line, creating redundancy.; Backup via sed -i.bak may not preserve SELinux contexts or extended attributes on some systems.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets FAIL_DELAY to a non‑zero value, uses a backup, and edits /etc/login.defs in a straightforward way that does not interfere with authentication mechanisms. The change is limited to a login configuration parameter and is unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Possible loss of SELinux context or extended attributes on /etc/login.defs due to sed -i replacement; Creation of a backup file /etc/login.defs.bak; Potential duplicate FAIL_DELAY entries if the file already contains multiple lines

title: Set Interactive Session Timeout
triage_reason: The remediation only edits shell profile files to set a positive TMOUT value, which is a standard STIG hardening measure. It does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical services, and the change is reversible. triage_estimated_impact: potential impact on container workloads that rely on user namespaces; kernel parameter modification
attempt_1_commands: grep -R "user.max_user_namespaces" /etc/sysctl.d 2>/dev/null || true
attempt_2_commands: sysctl -w user.max_user_namespaces=0; sysctl --system
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the kernel parameter at runtime, persists it via /etc/sysctl.d, and reloads sysctl, which will remediate the vulnerability. It lacks handling for container workloads and audit logging, but it will still resolve the issue on non‑container hosts.
review_concerns: Setting user.max_user_namespaces=0 may break containers that rely on user namespaces.; No audit logging or change tracking is performed.; No conditional handling for hosts that run containers (e.g., using a different config file).
qa_verdict_reason: The plan follows standard sysctl hardening practices, persists the setting via /etc/sysctl.d, and applies it without reboot. Disabling unprivileged user namespaces does not affect core system services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The only likely impact is on container runtimes or applications that rely on user namespaces, which is acceptable for non‑container hosts.
qa_side_effects: Container runtimes (e.g., Docker, Podman) may fail to start or run containers that require user namespaces; User‑level sandboxed applications that depend on unprivileged user namespaces could be impacted

title: Disable acquiring, saving, and processing core dumps
triage_reason: Masking the systemd-coredump.socket disables core dump processing without affecting authentication, networking, or critical services. It is a standard STIG hardening step and poses no risk of lockout.
triage_estimated_impact: loss of core dump debugging information
attempt_1_commands: systemctl list-unit-files --type socket | grep -q "systemd-coredump.socket" && echo exists || echo not_exists; systemctl is-system-running; systemctl stop systemd-coredump.socket; systemctl mask systemd-coredump.socket
review_feedback: The plan correctly stops and masks the systemd-coredump.socket unit, which disables core dump processing as required. It follows the recommended remediation steps and does not introduce any new risks.
qa_verdict_reason: Masking systemd-coredump.socket only disables core dump processing, which is not required for normal operation of critical services. It does not affect networking, authentication, or other essential daemons. The remediation follows the recommended script and introduces no configuration changes that could cause outages.
qa_side_effects: Core dumps will no longer be captured, limiting post‑mortem debugging information.

title: Disable core dump backtraces
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a systemd configuration file to set ProcessSizeMax=0, which disables backtrace generation for large core dumps. This is a straightforward file edit that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services, and it does not require a system reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential loss of detailed debugging information from core dumps; Minor risk of misconfiguration causing systemd-coredump service to fail if syntax is incorrect
attempt_1_commands: mkdir -p /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d; systemctl daemon-reload
review_feedback: The plan correctly targets the ProcessSizeMax setting in systemd core dump configuration and will disable backtrace generation for large core dumps as required. It handles the main configuration file and any drop‑in files, and reloads the daemon to apply changes.
review_concerns: The script may create duplicate ProcessSizeMax entries if the key already exists in a file but is commented out or appears multiple times.; No backup of the original configuration files is made before editing, which could hinder rollback if something goes wrong.; The plan does not verify that the modifications are effective after reload (e.g., using systemd-analyze cat-config).
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only edits systemd core dump configuration files to set ProcessSizeMax=0 and then reloads the systemd daemon. This does not affect SSH, authentication, networking, or other critical services. The change is appropriate for disabling backtrace generation and is unlikely to cause service outages.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate ProcessSizeMax entries if the key already exists in a file; No backup of original configuration files is made before editing

title: Disable storing core dump
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a systemd configuration file to set Storage=none, which is a simple, well‑understood change that does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem layout. It does not require a reboot and can be validated quickly.
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/systemd/coredump.conf /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.bak; sed -i 's/^#\?\s*Storage\s*=.*/Storage=none/' /etc/systemd/coredump.conf; systemctl daemon-reload
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies and modifies the relevant systemd coredump configuration to set Storage=none, and reloads the daemon. It will remediate the vulnerability. Minor improvements could be made for robustness and best‑practice compliance.
review_concerns: The plan does not explicitly back up configuration files before editing.; If the target file is missing, the plan mentions creating it but does not specify the exact contents (section header needed).; Potential for duplicate Storage lines if the script is run multiple times without checking for existing exact matches.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies the systemd coredump configuration to set Storage=none and reloads the daemon. This does not affect core services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The changes are reversible and do not introduce functional regressions.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate Storage lines if the script is run repeatedly without checking for exact matches; No backup of original configuration files is made

title: Disable Core Dumps for All Users
triage_reason: Disabling core dumps via PAM limits is a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect system boot or remote access. The change is limited to a configuration file and can be applied without risk of lockout.
attempt_1_commands: ls -1 /etc/security/limits.d; grep -R "\*\s\+hard\s\+core" /etc/security/limits.conf /etc/security/limits.d 2>/dev/null || true
review_feedback: The plan will add the required '* hard core 0' setting and comment out conflicting entries, which will disable core dumps for all users. Minor syntax issues and redundancy could be refined, but the overall approach is sound.
review_concerns: The sed command used to uncomment a commented line may not match all whitespace variations and could fail.; Appending the line to limits.conf while also creating a drop‑in file can lead to duplicate entries.; The plan does not explicitly reload PAM or inform the administrator that changes take effect at next login.; Commenting out non‑zero core limits in limits.d may miss lines that have different spacing or comments.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies PAM limits to disable core dumps, which is a non‑critical security setting. The changes are limited to /etc/security/limits.conf and /etc/security/limits.d/ and do not affect daemon configurations, networking, or authentication mechanisms. Even if duplicate entries are created, they simply enforce the same value (0) and will not cause service failures. No immediate service restarts are required; changes take effect on next login, so there is no risk of locking out SSH or other critical services.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate '* hard core 0' entries in limits.conf and drop‑in file; Requires users to re‑login for the new limit to take effect

title: Enable Randomized Layout of Virtual Address Space
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies a kernel sysctl parameter and persists it in /etc/sysctl.d, which is a standard, low‑impact configuration change. It does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem layout and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: minimal impact on running services; no change to authentication or network settings
attempt_1_commands: ls /etc/sysctl.d; grep -R "kernel.randomize_va_space" /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; sysctl -w kernel.randomize_va_space=2; sysctl --system
review_feedback: The plan will set kernel.randomize_va_space=2 at runtime and persistently, which addresses the vulnerability. Minor improvements can be made for robustness and compliance with best practices.
review_concerns: The plan does not reload the sysctl configuration after writing the new file, so the change will not take effect until a reboot unless sysctl -w is used for each setting.; It mentions a 'read_file' operation that may not be a standard shell command, potentially causing a failure if not defined.; Potential duplicate entries could arise if multiple files under /etc/sysctl.d contain the parameter and the script does not handle all cases consistently.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting kernel.randomize_va_space to 2 is a standard hardening measure that does not affect critical services. The plan correctly updates or creates a sysctl config file and applies the runtime change, with minimal risk of service interruption or lockout.

title: Enable page allocator poisoning

title: Disable KDump Kernel Crash Analyzer (kdump)
triage_reason: Disabling the kdump service only stops crash‑dump collection. It does not affect authentication, networking, or core system functionality, and the remediation consists of standard systemctl commands. No risk of losing SSH/console access is introduced.
attempt_1_commands: systemctl list-unit-files kdump.socket; /usr/bin/systemctl stop kdump.service; /usr/bin/systemctl disable kdump.service; /usr/bin/systemctl mask kdump.service; /usr/bin/systemctl reset-failed kdump.service
review_feedback: The plan correctly stops, disables, and masks the kdump service and its socket, which will satisfy the OpenSCAP requirement. However, it could be improved by handling edge cases such as resetting a failed state and ensuring commands run safely on offline systems.
review_concerns: No reset-failed command, which may leave the service in a failed state and cause OVAL checks to fail.; Conditional handling of the socket is not explicitly defined; the plan assumes the existence check will be parsed correctly.; Uses generic 'systemctl' without specifying full path, which could be an issue on some hardened environments.; Does not verify system state (online/offline) before attempting to stop services.
qa_verdict_reason: Disabling and masking kdump and its socket only removes crash dump functionality, which is not required for normal operation and does not affect critical services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The commands are standard systemctl operations and will not lock out access or cause data loss.
qa_side_effects: Loss of kernel crash dump collection capability, making post‑crash debugging more difficult

title: Verify Permissions on cron.d
triage_reason: The remediation only changes directory permissions for /etc/cron.d, which is a standard file permission hardening step with no impact on authentication, networking, or critical services.
attempt_1_commands: ls -ld /etc/cron.d; chmod 0700 /etc/cron.d; ls -ld /etc/cron.d
review_feedback: The plan correctly verifies and then sets the /etc/cron.d directory permissions to 0700, which addresses the identified vulnerability.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing /etc/cron.d permissions to 0700 restricts access to root only, which is appropriate for a system cron directory. It does not affect the cron daemon's ability to read the files (runs as root) and does not impact SSH, networking, or other critical services.
qa_side_effects: Non‑root users will no longer be able to list or read the /etc/cron.d directory, but this is expected for security hardening.

title: Verify Permissions on cron.weekly

title: Install fapolicyd Package

The package does not modify existing services on install and its default policy is permissive, so it is unlikely to interfere with sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The plan follows the vendor recommendation and includes a pre‑check to avoid reinstalling an already present package.

title: Enable the File Access Policy Service
triage_reason: Enabling the fapolicyd service is a straightforward service enablement with no impact on authentication, networking, or filesystems, and it does not risk lockout or critical service disruption.
attempt_3_commands: rpm -q fapolicyd; /usr/bin/systemctl unmask fapolicyd.service; /usr/bin/systemctl enable fapolicyd.service; /usr/bin/systemctl start fapolicyd.service

title: Configure Fapolicy Module to Employ a Deny-all, Permit-by-exception Policy to Allow the Execution of Authorized Software Programs.
triage_reason: The remediation creates a deny-all rule for fapolicyd and disables permissive mode, which can prevent execution of essential system binaries (e.g., sshd, bash) if proper exceptions are not added. This poses a clear risk of loss of console/SSH access or service disruption, requiring manual verification and planning.
