title: Configure System to Forward All Mail For The Root Account
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/aliases and runs newaliases, which is a straightforward configuration change with no impact on authentication, network services, or system boot. It does not risk SSH or console lockout.
attempt_1_commands: sed -i -r 's/^#?\s*root:.*/root: root@example.com/' /etc/aliases; grep -q '^\s*root:' /etc/aliases || echo 'root: root@example.com' >> /etc/aliases; newaliases
attempt_2_commands: newaliases; cp /etc/aliases /etc/aliases.bak
attempt_3_commands: sed -E -i 's/^\s*#?\s*root\s*:.*$/root: admin@example.com/' /etc/aliases; cp /etc/aliases /etc/aliases.bak.$(date +%s); postalias /etc/aliases; systemctl reload postfix; systemctl status postfix
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up /etc/aliases, updates or adds a root alias, rebuilds the alias database, and reloads Postfix, which will forward root mail to the specified address and satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. Minor improvements are needed but the fix is functional.
review_concerns: The placeholder email address (admin@example.com) must be replaced with a real monitored address before deployment.; No explicit error handling or exit‑status checks for postalias and systemctl reload, which could leave the system in an inconsistent state if they fail.; The plan mentions appending the root line if missing but does not provide the exact echo command.; No validation of the email address syntax or reachability is performed.; Reliance on the postalias command assumes it is installed; fallback to newaliases could be considered.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/aliases, rebuilds the alias database, and reloads Postfix. These actions are standard for mail alias configuration and do not interfere with SSH, auditd, firewalld, networking, or other critical services. A backup is created for rollback, and the changes are limited to Postfix, making the plan safe.
qa_side_effects: temporary mail delivery delay while Postfix reloads; possible bounce if the specified email address is invalid or unreachable

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation only edits NTP/Chrony configuration files to add or adjust the maxpoll parameter, which is a straightforward text substitution without affecting authentication, boot process, or critical services.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; configuration drift; potential time sync in if misconfigured
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q ntpd; rpm -q chrony; ls /etc/chrony.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || echo none; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -E '/^\s*[^#]\s*(server|pool|peer)\b/ { /maxpoll/ { s/(maxpoll\s+)[0-9]+/\1 4/ } ; /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 4/ }' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i '/^\s*[^#]\s*pool\b/ { /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 4/ }' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i '/^\s*pool\b/ { /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 4/ }' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the relevant NTP/Chrony configuration files and uses sed to enforce a maxpoll value of 4 on all server, pool, and peer directives. This will address the vulnerability if executed. However, the plan could be strengthened by adding backups, handling comments more robustly, and restarting the service to apply changes.
review_concerns: No backup of configuration files is made before in‑place editing.; The plan does not restart ntpd or chronyd after modifying the config, so changes may not take effect until a manual restart.; The sed expression may miss lines that contain inline comments or extra whitespace, potentially leaving some directives without maxpoll.; If both ntpd and chrony are installed, the plan does not clearly define which configuration to modify.; Using read_file/write_file in conjunction with sed -i may be redundant.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits NTP/Chrony configuration files to enforce a maxpoll value. It does not touch critical services such as sshd, firewalld, or auditd, and the changes are limited to server/pool/peer directives. While a backup and service restart would be advisable, the modifications are unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Potential NTP/Chrony service restart needed for changes to take effect; If the sed command mishandles unusual line formatting or inline comments, the config could become syntactically invalid, preventing the time service from starting

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The fix only requires editing the chrony configuration file to remove non-server directives and restarting the chronyd service, which is a low‑impact change with no risk of locking out users.
attempt_2_commands: cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -r -e 's/^(\s*)pool/#\1pool/' -e 's/^(\s*)makestep/#\1makestep/' -e 's/^(\s*)maxdelay/#\1maxdelay/' -e 's/^(\s*)minpoll/#\1minpoll/' -e 's/^(\s*)maxpoll/#\1maxpoll/' -e 's/^(\s*)stratumweight/#\1stratumweight/' -e 's/^(\s*)rtcsync/#\1rtcsync/' -e 's/^(\s*)ntpsync/#\1ntpsync/' -e 's/^(\s*)hwtimestamp/#\1hwtimestamp/' -e 's/^(\s*)leapsectz/#\1leapsectz/' /etc/chrony.conf; chronyd -Q; sed -i -r 's/^#\s*pool\s+(.*)/server \1/' /etc/chrony.conf; chronyd -Q; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan addresses the core issue by backing up the config, removing non‑server time‑source directives, validating syntax, and restarting chronyd. It preserves essential directives (driftfile, logdir, allow, keyfile, local) and includes a validation step, so it will resolve the vulnerability if executed correctly.
review_concerns: Sed commands may unintentionally comment out lines that are already commented or contain the target word in a different context.; The plan does not explicitly handle idempotence; repeated runs could add extra comment characters.; No mention of testing the service after restart (e.g., checking synchronization status).
qa_verdict_reason: The plan follows a standard hardening approach: backs up the config, comments out only non‑server time‑source directives, validates syntax, and restarts chronyd. It does not modify unrelated services and includes a syntax check before restart, minimizing risk of service outage. The main risk is loss of time synchronization if no server directives remain, which could affect time‑dependent services but will not lock out SSH or break critical daemons.
qa_side_effects: Potential loss of accurate system time if no server directives are left after commenting out pool directives; Short‑term time drift may affect services that rely on precise time (e.g., Kerberos, log timestamps); Chronyd restart may cause a brief period where NTP is unavailable

title: Disable Host-Based Authentication
triage_reason: The remediation only requires setting a standard SSH configuration option to disable host-based authentication, which is already disabled by default. This change is low‑risk, does not affect critical services, and follows typical STIG hardening patterns.
attempt_1_commands: touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf; chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf; sed -i 's/^\s*#\?\s*HostbasedAuthentication\s\+.*/HostbasedAuthentication no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf; grep -q '^\s*HostbasedAuthentication' /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf; echo 'HostbasedAuthentication no' >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf; systemctl reload sshd
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set HostbasedAuthentication no in the target config file and set appropriate permissions. However, it could be improved by ensuring the file exists before editing, handling errors, and reloading the sshd service to apply the change immediately.
review_concerns: If /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf does not exist, the sed command will fail.; The plan does not reload or restart sshd, so the new setting may not take effect until a manual restart.; The grep check will match any occurrence, including commented lines, which could skip the append step even if the active setting is missing.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits a specific SSH include file to ensure HostbasedAuthentication is set to 'no', using safe in‑place substitution and appending if needed. It does not modify other settings, and permissions are set to the standard 0600. No services are stopped or restarted, so the change will not cause immediate outage; the new setting will take effect after the next sshd reload. The only minor risk is that the target file may not exist, which would cause the sed command to fail but would not break sshd.
qa_side_effects: If /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf does not exist, the sed command will error and the change will not be applied.; The change will not be active until sshd is reloaded or restarted.

title: Disable Compression Or Set Compression to delayed
triage_reason: The remediation only edits SSH daemon configuration files to disable or delay compression, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not alter bootloader, kernel, or partitioning and is unlikely to cause loss of access if tested before deployment.
attempt_1_commands: grep -n "Compression" -n /etc/ssh/sshd_config; sed -i 's/^\s*#\s*Compression\s\+.*/Compression delayed/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config; grep -n "Compression" -n /etc/ssh/sshd_config

The plan checks for existing installation and uses the package manager correctly, so it is unlikely to cause outages or lock‑outs.
qa_side_effects: Adds the usbguard daemon and systemd unit; May require post‑install configuration to define USB device policies; Potentially restricts USB device usage if the service is enabled
